# ARLO Songwriting Craft — Prompt Rows

51 prompt rows for the Songwriting Craft & Workflows stream. **15 of the 51 (Bucket A) produce dual output** — both a corpus chunk file AND a workflow template at a new path, `arlo/workflows/<slug>.md`. The Master Template at the top of `PROMPTS.md` applies to all rows; this file adds stream-specific clauses + per-bucket frontmatter specs.

**Provenance note:** Files generated by these prompts will be tagged `Deep-research synthesis (2026-05) — Songwriting Craft` so that future verification passes can spot them.

---

## Stream-specific master template addendum

Every prompt in this stream operates under the standard ARLO master template (see `PROMPTS.md`) with these additional clauses inserted into the **Content requirements** block:

> **North-star check.** Every section of the file must be evaluable against the question: *does this help a stuck musician get unstuck and finish a song?* If a section is academic music theory without an actionable songwriting move, cut it.
>
> **Source preference.** Prefer named working practitioners with citable interviews/books: Pat Pattison, Jimmy Webb, Sheila Davis, Paul Zollo (*Songwriters on Songwriting*), Rick Rubin (*The Creative Act*), David Lynch (*Catching the Big Fish*), Brian Eno (*A Year With Swollen Appendices*), Sodajerker podcast, Tape Notes, Switched On Pop, Strong Songs, Rick Beato's *What Makes This Song Great*, Adam Neely. **Refuse to cite** AI-generated "songwriting tips" sites, generic listicles, or genre-fan blogs without verifiable authorial expertise.
>
> **Honesty about subjectivity.** Craft is more contested than DAW documentation. When a technique is one school of thought, name the school. When working writers disagree, surface the disagreement. When a "rule" is genre-bound, name the genre. Prefer "Pattison teaches X" over "the right way is X."
>
> **Cross-link the existing corpus.** Before writing, search `corpus/` for the related general-corpus file. If found, end with a `See also:` footer listing the relative path. Examples: `corpus/lyrics/object-writing-and-sensory-imagery.md`, `corpus/harmony/modal-interchange-and-borrowed-chords.md`, `corpus/structure/section-function-verse-prechorus-chorus-bridge.md`.
>
> **Default tags.** Every file gets `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, plus the bucket tag (`workflow-recipe`, `stall-recovery`, `lyric-craft`, `melodic-harmonic`, `form-decision`, or `finishing-psychology`), plus topic and practitioner tags.

---

## Bucket-specific clauses

### Bucket A — Workflow Recipes — DUAL OUTPUT (read carefully)

Each Bucket-A prompt writes TWO files. The agent must produce BOTH.

**File 1: corpus chunk** at `/Users/cfitt/Dev/arlo/corpus/songwriting/workflows/<slug>.md`

Standard ARLO chunk format (1500–2500 words):

```
# <Title>

**Scope:** songwriting
**Source:** Deep-research synthesis (2026-05) — Songwriting Craft — verify before treating as authoritative
**Canonical references:** <list>
**Tags:** songwriting-craft, finishing-songs, workflow-recipe, <practitioner>, <topic tags>

---

[8–15 H2 sections of 100–250 words each — premise, history, what it actually looks like, when it fits, common failure modes, variations, etc.]

## Cited Retrieval Topics
[5–10 conversational query phrases]

## Sources
[markdown-linked URLs of every citation]

See also: <relative paths to general-corpus files this builds on>
```

**File 2: workflow template** at `/Users/cfitt/Dev/arlo/arlo/workflows/<slug>.md`

Shorter (target 600–1000 words), operational, with the required frontmatter:

```
---
name: <slug>
origin: <person or tradition>
type: lyrics-first | music-first | beat-first | production-first | exploring
applicable_when: "<one-line trigger condition — the state of the user/song when this workflow fits>"
duration_estimate: "<rough wall-clock — e.g., '30 min for first pass', '2 sessions', 'one writing day'>"
---

# Workflow: <human-readable name>

## The premise
[2-3 sentences. What this workflow believes about how songs get made.]

## When ARLO should offer this
[The trigger condition expanded — when ARLO's user-stuck detector should surface this workflow as an option.]

## The steps
[Numbered, operational. Each step is something the user does. 5–10 steps typically.]

## ARLO prompts for each step (verbatim)
[Per-step Socratic prompts ARLO uses VERBATIM. These should be ready to copy-paste into ARLO's conversation. One block per step.]

## When to abandon this workflow
[Signals that this workflow isn't fitting — what should make the user (or ARLO) switch to a different approach.]

## Sources
[The same citations as the corpus file, formatted as a list.]
```

The two files share sourcing but differ in *function*: the corpus file is what ARLO retrieves to *explain* the workflow; the template is what ARLO *executes* when the user opts in.

### Bucket B — Stalling-Point Recovery — frontmatter spec

Each Bucket-B corpus file uses this frontmatter additionally to the standard:

```
---
problem: "<short name of the stall>"
common_signals:
  - "<phrase the user might type when this stall is happening>"
  - "<another phrase>"
  - "<...>"
recovery_moves: <int>
---
```

Body shape:

```
# Stall: <name>

**Scope:** songwriting
**Source:** Deep-research synthesis (2026-05) — Songwriting Craft — verify before treating as authoritative
**Canonical references:** <list>
**Tags:** songwriting-craft, finishing-songs, stall-recovery, <stall-specific tag>

---

## Why it stalls
[Diagnosis. What's actually happening when this stall hits. Schools-of-thought welcome.]

## The <N> recovery moves
[Each move is one H3. Each move is concrete and named. Format: 1-2 paragraphs per move.]

## Socratic prompts ARLO uses verbatim
[5–10 question-form prompts ARLO can ask. These are ready-to-paste. Examples:
- "What was the song about when you started? Read me the first verse."
- "If you weren't allowed to add anything, what would you cut?"]

## When to abandon and re-arrange
[The "stop trying to fix this and try the next song" signal.]

## Sources
[markdown links]

## Cited Retrieval Topics
[5–10 conversational queries that should retrieve this stall playbook]

See also: <cross-link paths>
```

### Buckets C, D, E, F — standard ARLO chunk shape

Standard front-matter (Scope / Source / Canonical references / Tags), 8–15 H2 sections of 100–250 words, Cited Retrieval Topics, Sources, See-also.

---

## A. Workflow Recipes — `corpus/songwriting/workflows/` + `arlo/workflows/`

### A1. `nick-cave-letter-method`

- **Title:** Nick Cave's Letter Method — Writing a Song as a Letter to a Real Person
- **Slug:** `nick-cave-letter-method`
- **Origin:** Nick Cave
- **Type:** lyrics-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User has a strong emotion attached to a real person/event but the lyric keeps coming out vague or universal"
- **Duration estimate:** "60–90 min for first draft; second pass next session"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (write the song as a letter to a specific person — the song hides inside the letter)
  - Cave's own description on The Red Hand Files
  - the "no abstractions, no universals" discipline
  - what to do when the letter is finished (extract the verses, find the chorus inside)
  - when this workflow fits (emotionally loaded subject, vague drafts) and when it doesn't (purely sonic exploration)
  - the address question (writing to someone who'll never read it vs. someone who will)
- **Canonical references:** Nick Cave, *The Red Hand Files* (his letter-style newsletter); Cave interviews in *Faith, Hope and Carnage* (Cave + Seán O'Hagan); Sodajerker on Cave (if episode exists, verify)
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `cave`, `letter-method`, `lyrics-first`, `emotional-specificity`

### A2. `david-lynch-catching-big-fish`

- **Title:** David Lynch's "Catching the Big Fish" — Meditative Idea-Fishing
- **Slug:** `david-lynch-catching-big-fish`
- **Origin:** David Lynch
- **Type:** exploring
- **Applicable_when:** "User can't generate ideas / feels creatively dry / is forcing material that isn't there"
- **Duration estimate:** "20 min daily practice over a week; results emerge over time"
- **Coverage points:**
  - Lynch's *Catching the Big Fish* premise — ideas live deep; meditation lets you fish for them
  - the practice (TM-based meditation; daily; no scheduled creative output)
  - what counts as "a fish" (a fragment, an image, a phrase — not a finished song)
  - the capture discipline (notebook by the bed; no editing in the moment)
  - applying to songwriting (Lynch is a filmmaker; how working musicians have adapted the practice)
  - when this fits (dry spell, forcing-it phase) and when it doesn't (you already have material to develop)
- **Canonical references:** David Lynch, *Catching the Big Fish: Meditation, Consciousness, and Creativity*; Lynch's David Lynch Foundation talks
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `lynch`, `meditation`, `exploring`, `ideation`

### A3. `eno-oblique-strategies`

- **Title:** Brian Eno's Oblique Strategies — Constraint Cards as Stall-Breaker
- **Slug:** `eno-oblique-strategies`
- **Origin:** Brian Eno + Peter Schmidt (1975)
- **Type:** exploring
- **Applicable_when:** "User is stuck in a known pattern / making the same choice every time / needs an external nudge"
- **Duration estimate:** "5–15 min per card; integrates into existing session"
- **Coverage points:**
  - origin (Eno + Schmidt, 1975, ~100 cards)
  - the premise (deliberately oblique instructions force lateral moves)
  - example cards (verified from official sources, not invented)
  - how Eno actually uses them (the Bowie *Low* sessions, the U2 *Joshua Tree* sessions, the Talking Heads *Remain in Light* sessions — cross-link albums-stream files)
  - how a songwriter (not producer) can adapt them
  - the "draw and obey" rule vs. "draw and consider"
  - digital/app implementations and whether they preserve the practice
- **Canonical references:** Brian Eno + Peter Schmidt, *Oblique Strategies* (official deck); Eno's *A Year With Swollen Appendices*; Eno interviews on the deck's making
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `eno`, `oblique-strategies`, `constraint`, `exploring`

### A4. `bowie-burroughs-cut-up`

- **Title:** Bowie's Cut-Up Technique — Burroughs-Derived Lyric Generation
- **Slug:** `bowie-burroughs-cut-up`
- **Origin:** David Bowie (William S. Burroughs–derived)
- **Type:** lyrics-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User's lyrics feel too linear / too 'on the nose' / lacks the irrational image juxtaposition that lifts pop into poetry"
- **Duration estimate:** "45–60 min for first pass; iterate over multiple sessions"
- **Coverage points:**
  - origin (Burroughs cut-up technique, 1950s; Bowie adopted in 1970s)
  - the mechanic (write paragraphs, cut into strips, rearrange)
  - Bowie's own description (Verbasizer software in the 90s — Bowie's digitization of the practice)
  - which Bowie songs were cut-up (*Diamond Dogs*, *Hallo Spaceboy*, parts of *Blackstar*)
  - how a modern writer can do it (paper, app, AI as cut-up partner)
  - the editing pass after the cut (most output is unusable; the gems are surprises)
  - when this fits (linear lyrics, on-the-nose drafts) and when it doesn't (you have a story you want to tell straight)
- **Canonical references:** William Burroughs + Brion Gysin, *The Third Mind*; Bowie interviews in Paul Trynka's *Starman*; Verbasizer documentation; Pitchfork's Bowie cut-up retrospective
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `bowie`, `burroughs`, `cut-up`, `lyrics-first`

### A5. `rubin-reduction-philosophy`

- **Title:** Rick Rubin's Reduction Philosophy — Subtraction as the Production Move
- **Slug:** `rubin-reduction-philosophy`
- **Origin:** Rick Rubin
- **Type:** exploring
- **Applicable_when:** "User's song is overproduced / cluttered / lost its core / too many parts fighting each other"
- **Duration estimate:** "one session of subtraction; bounce and listen"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (the song's essence is buried under additions; subtraction reveals it)
  - Rubin's own description in *The Creative Act* (chapters on subtraction)
  - the Slayer *Reign in Blood* example (Rubin stripped layers)
  - the Johnny Cash *American Recordings* example (voice + guitar; nothing else)
  - how a songwriter does this without Rubin in the room (mute every track and add back one at a time)
  - the "what is this song actually about" stress test
  - when this fits (cluttered demo, lost focus) and when it doesn't (you haven't laid out enough material yet)
- **Canonical references:** Rick Rubin, *The Creative Act: A Way of Being*; Rubin interview in *Broken Record* podcast (Rubin's own podcast); Rubin's *60 Minutes* feature
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `rubin`, `reduction`, `subtraction`, `exploring`

### A6. `waits-break-the-demo`

- **Title:** Tom Waits' "Break the Demo" — Breaking the Polished Version to Find the Song
- **Slug:** `waits-break-the-demo`
- **Origin:** Tom Waits
- **Type:** production-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User has a polished demo that feels lifeless / safe / 'correct' in a way that's killing it"
- **Duration estimate:** "one session of deliberate sabotage; A/B against the polished version"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (the polished demo is the enemy; break it to find what's interesting)
  - Waits' production with Kathleen Brennan (*Swordfishtrombones* onward — the break from the early polished records)
  - the broken-instrument and unconventional-mic discipline
  - applying to a digital workflow (deliberate bitcrushing, deliberate tape-warble, deliberate wrong-mic-position)
  - the "interesting beats correct" stress test
  - when this fits (lifeless polish) and when it doesn't (the polish IS the song — modern pop)
- **Canonical references:** Tom Waits interview archive (*Wire*, *Mojo*, *The Guardian*); Brennan + Waits production notes referenced in *Lowside of the Road* (Barney Hoskyns biography)
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `waits`, `break-the-demo`, `production-first`, `unpolished`

### A7. `aimee-mann-specificity-first`

- **Title:** Aimee Mann's Specificity-First — Single Image, Excavate Outward
- **Slug:** `aimee-mann-specificity-first`
- **Origin:** Aimee Mann
- **Type:** lyrics-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User has a feeling but no song / wants to write about something abstract (loss, longing, change) and can't find the way in"
- **Duration estimate:** "60 min to find the image; multiple sessions to excavate"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (one concrete image → excavate the song from the image; abstractions get earned by the time you finish, not declared)
  - Mann's own description in Sodajerker interviews and other long-form
  - examples from her catalog ("Save Me", "Wise Up", *Mental Illness* album as case study)
  - the "noun-verb-noun" diagnostic (every line should have a concrete noun, an action verb, and another concrete noun)
  - what to do when the image runs out (find an adjacent image, not a more abstract claim)
  - when this fits (abstract feeling, vague drafts) and when it doesn't (you have a story arc already)
- **Canonical references:** Sodajerker on Songwriting — Aimee Mann episode(s); Mann interviews in *The Talkhouse*; Paul Zollo's interview with Mann in *Songwriters on Songwriting*
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `mann`, `specificity`, `concrete-image`, `lyrics-first`

### A8. `beat-first-topline-workflow`

- **Title:** Beat-First / Topline-Over-Beat — The Hip-Hop and Modern Pop Workflow
- **Slug:** `beat-first-topline-workflow`
- **Origin:** Hip-hop tradition; modern pop topline writers (Max Martin school)
- **Type:** beat-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User is a topline writer or producer-songwriter; the music exists first and the lyric/melody comes over it"
- **Duration estimate:** "30 min for first topline pass; iterate"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the workflow (producer builds a beat / track; topline writer adds melody + lyric over it)
  - the hip-hop origin (beat-then-rhyme tradition going back to early hip-hop)
  - the modern pop adaptation (Max Martin's "melodic math"; the Cheiron / Stargate / Shellback chain)
  - the toplining session — what actually happens in the room
  - the "find the pocket" discipline (vocal rhythm relative to the beat's pocket)
  - syllable-count constraint as a feature (the beat dictates phrase length)
  - when this fits (you have a track, you need a song; you write better against constraints) and when it doesn't (lyric-driven story songs)
- **Canonical references:** John Seabrook, *The Song Machine: Inside the Hit Factory*; Switched On Pop episodes on Max Martin / melodic math; Sodajerker on topline writers
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `beat-first`, `topline`, `max-martin`, `hip-hop`, `pop`

### A9. `stems-first-production-first`

- **Title:** Stems-First / Production-First — Build the World, Write Last
- **Slug:** `stems-first-production-first`
- **Origin:** Modern bedroom-producer school (Bon Iver, Frank Ocean, James Blake era)
- **Type:** production-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User has a strong sonic instinct / wants to build a world first / writing a song into existing music feels backwards"
- **Duration estimate:** "multiple sessions; world first, song extracted later"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (build the production world — chords, textures, drums, atmospheres; the song reveals itself inside the world)
  - the bedroom-producer evolution (DAW-native composition; the loop as starting point)
  - Bon Iver *22, A Million* as case study (cross-link the Bon Iver Aesthetic-stream files)
  - Frank Ocean *Blonde* as case study (cross-link Aesthetic stream)
  - James Blake's process
  - the risk (production lock-in — you commit to a production before the song earns it)
  - the "what is this song saying" check after the world is built
  - when this fits (sonic-led writers, electronic and indie producers) and when it doesn't (acoustic singer-songwriter material)
- **Canonical references:** Sound on Sound Inside Track features on Bon Iver (cross-link `corpus/artists/bon-iver-*` if/when written); Tape Notes podcast episodes on production-first writers
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `production-first`, `bedroom-producer`, `bon-iver`, `blake`, `ocean`

### A10. `tom-cosm-8-bar-loop-cycle`

- **Title:** Tom Cosm's 8-Bar Loop Cycle — The Bedroom-Producer Discipline
- **Slug:** `tom-cosm-8-bar-loop-cycle`
- **Origin:** Tom Cosm / Ill Gates (loop-based pre-production school)
- **Type:** production-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User is in Session-view / loop-based DAW mode and needs a discipline to stay in the loop until it's undeniable"
- **Duration estimate:** "one session per loop; days/weeks per song"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (work only with an 8-bar loop until it's undeniable; arrangement comes later)
  - the "if it's not exciting in 8 bars, it won't be in 8 minutes" rule
  - Cosm's tutorials and Ill Gates writings on the discipline
  - the iteration cycle (mute / unmute; subtract / add; trade roles between instruments)
  - the "undeniable" stress test (would you keep listening if you found this on someone else's track?)
  - knowing when to break out into arrangement (the looping-forever trap)
  - **cross-link `corpus/daw/ableton/timeless/eight-bars-first-discipline.md`** — that file covers the Session-view mechanics; this file covers the songwriting decision
- **Canonical references:** Tom Cosm "8 Bars" tutorial series (YouTube — verify and timestamp); Ill Gates writings on loop discipline; Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music* (decide-once chapters)
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `cosm`, `ill-gates`, `loop`, `production-first`

### A11. `spoon-two-demos-a-week`

- **Title:** Spoon's Two-Demos-a-Week — Forcing Function as Songwriting Discipline
- **Slug:** `spoon-two-demos-a-week`
- **Origin:** Britt Daniel / Spoon
- **Type:** music-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User is in a barren patch / hasn't finished a song in months / needs a forcing function to break the procrastination loop"
- **Duration estimate:** "two demos per week, two-week minimum to see the effect"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (commit to finishing N demos per week regardless of quality; the discipline of the deadline beats the quest for the masterpiece)
  - Daniel's own description in interviews about the *They Want My Soul* / *Hot Thoughts* era
  - the "demo, not master" definition (the demo is finished when it's structurally a song; not when it's mixed)
  - what counts as a demo (the user defines; the bar should be uncomfortably low)
  - the weekly review pass (listen to what you made; pick what to develop, archive the rest)
  - the "throwaway song per week" practice from general-corpus workflow (cross-link)
  - when this fits (procrastination, perfectionism) and when it doesn't (you're producing an album with quality constraints)
- **Canonical references:** Britt Daniel interviews in *Pitchfork*, *Stereogum*; Sodajerker on Spoon (verify episode); Steven Hyden's *Twilight of the Gods* contains the Spoon section
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `spoon`, `forcing-function`, `discipline`, `music-first`

### A12. `joni-mitchell-alternate-tuning-first`

- **Title:** Joni Mitchell's Alternate-Tuning-First — The Tuning IS the Song
- **Slug:** `joni-mitchell-alternate-tuning-first`
- **Origin:** Joni Mitchell
- **Type:** music-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User is a guitarist whose songs all sound similar / stuck in standard-tuning chord shapes / needs new harmonic territory"
- **Duration estimate:** "60 min to find a tuning; multiple sessions to write into it"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (a new tuning produces unfamiliar chord shapes which produce unfamiliar music; the tuning is the seed)
  - Joni's catalog of tunings (she used 50+; *Blue*, *Hejira*, *Hissing of Summer Lawns* all use different tunings extensively)
  - canonical Joni tunings to try (open D modal, the *Hejira* tunings, DADGAD-adjacent)
  - the "shape, not chord" approach (find a beautiful shape; name the chord later, if ever)
  - the challenge of capo + tuning (transposition complications)
  - the "tuning is the song" stress test (if you re-tuned and re-played, would it be the same song?)
  - when this fits (guitarists in a rut, harmonically) and when it doesn't (writers who don't play guitar; pianists)
- **Canonical references:** *Joni Mitchell: In Her Own Words* (Malka Marom); JoniMitchell.com tunings database; Acoustic Guitar magazine archive on Joni tunings
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `joni`, `alternate-tuning`, `guitar`, `music-first`

### A13. `dylan-notebook-cassette-discipline`

- **Title:** Bob Dylan's Notebook + Cassette Discipline — Capture Everything, Edit Later
- **Slug:** `dylan-notebook-cassette-discipline`
- **Origin:** Bob Dylan
- **Type:** lyrics-first
- **Applicable_when:** "User has fleeting ideas that disappear / ideas come during walks/showers/driving / no capture discipline yet"
- **Duration estimate:** "daily ongoing practice; sit-down sessions monthly"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (every line, every melody, every dream — capture it; don't edit in the capture phase)
  - Dylan's actual practice (the notebooks at MoPOP / Tulsa Bob Dylan Archive; the cassette dictation discipline)
  - what counts as worth capturing (everything — the editing is a later, separate step)
  - the modern equivalent (voice memos, phone notes, the discipline of opening the app immediately)
  - the periodic review pass (sit with the notebook + cassettes once a week or month; find the gems)
  - examples from Dylan's archive (the *Blood on the Tracks* notebook; "Tangled Up in Blue" through multiple drafts)
  - when this fits (loose, idea-rich writer) and when it doesn't (you already have more material than you can use)
- **Canonical references:** *The Bob Dylan Archive* (Tulsa); *The Lyrics: 1961–2012*; David Hajdu's *Positively 4th Street*; Dylan interviews with Cameron Crowe (*Rolling Stone*)
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `dylan`, `capture-discipline`, `notebook`, `lyrics-first`

### A14. `sufjan-research-then-write`

- **Title:** Sufjan Stevens' Research-Then-Write — Album-as-Project Model
- **Slug:** `sufjan-research-then-write`
- **Origin:** Sufjan Stevens
- **Type:** exploring
- **Applicable_when:** "User wants to make a thematic record / has a topic they want to write about but no songs yet"
- **Duration estimate:** "weeks to months — research phase precedes writing"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the premise (deep research on a topic produces specific imagery and emotional connections that direct-write-from-self can't)
  - Sufjan's actual practice (*Illinois* was researched — Steakum, Lincoln, Jacksonville; *Carrie & Lowell* was internal research / grief work)
  - the discipline (read, take notes, write images — don't write songs yet)
  - the threshold (when do you have enough research to start writing?)
  - the risk (research becomes procrastination)
  - the "make the personal universal via the specific" rule
  - when this fits (album projects, conceptual writers) and when it doesn't (writing from immediate feeling)
- **Canonical references:** Sufjan interviews in *Pitchfork*, *Paste*, *The Believer*; *Sufjan Stevens' Illinoise* Asthmatic Kitty notes; the Carrie & Lowell project documentation
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `sufjan`, `research`, `album-as-project`, `exploring`

### A15. `nashville-co-writing-protocol`

- **Title:** Nashville Co-Writing Protocol — Music Row Standard Practice
- **Slug:** `nashville-co-writing-protocol`
- **Origin:** Nashville Music Row standard practice
- **Type:** varies (co-write)
- **Applicable_when:** "User is co-writing for the first time / has a co-write session scheduled and doesn't know the rules of engagement"
- **Duration estimate:** "3-hour session per song; one song per session"
- **Coverage points:**
  - the room (typically 2–3 writers; one session = one song; 3-hour rough cap)
  - roles (top-liner, hooker, lyricist; or rotating)
  - the pre-write (titles list, hook list, sometimes a track to write to)
  - the "no bad ideas" opening followed by the "kill your darlings" close
  - the split-sheet protocol (writers' percentages signed at end of session)
  - veto power and build-on convention (any writer can veto a line; the proposer can ask "what if instead...")
  - taking the song home vs. finishing in the room
  - what to do when the session is going badly (when to call it; when to push through)
  - the publisher and tape-day downstream
- **Canonical references:** Jason Blume, *Six Steps to Songwriting Success*; Holly Gleason archive; Nashville Songwriters Association International (NSAI) materials; Sodajerker on Nashville writers (Bobby Braddock, Liz Rose episodes if available)
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `workflow-recipe`, `co-writing`, `nashville`, `music-row`, `protocol`

---

## B. Stalling-Point Recovery — `corpus/songwriting/stalls/`

### B1. `the-bridge-problem`

- **Title:** Stall: The Bridge Problem
- **Common_signals:**
  - "I don't know what to do for the bridge"
  - "the bridge isn't working"
  - "the song needs a bridge but I don't have one"
  - "my bridge is just a repeat of the verse"
- **Recovery_moves:** 6
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (most bridges fail because they don't contrast — they're a third verse)
  - move 1: lyrical contrast (zoom out from the verse's perspective; introduce the missing party; address the listener directly)
  - move 2: harmonic contrast (modulate, borrow, or rest on a single chord)
  - move 3: melodic contrast (move to a different register; invert the verse contour)
  - move 4: instrumentation contrast (drop or add a key element)
  - move 5: the no-bridge option (some songs don't need one — when to cut it entirely)
  - move 6: the bridge-as-tag move (bridge IS the chorus repeated with a key change or stripped arrangement)
  - Socratic prompts (5–10 verbatim, ready for ARLO)
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *Lyric Form and Structure*; Jack Perricone, *Great Songwriting Techniques*; Switched On Pop bridge analyses; cross-link `corpus/structure/section-function-verse-prechorus-chorus-bridge.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `bridge`, `arrangement`

### B2. `the-second-verse-problem`

- **Title:** Stall: The Second Verse Problem
- **Common_signals:**
  - "I have a verse and chorus but the second verse is the same as the first"
  - "I don't know what to say in the second verse"
  - "my second verse is just filler"
  - "the song needs another verse but I have nothing"
- **Recovery_moves:** 6
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (the first verse set up the situation; the second has to *develop* it, not restate)
  - move 1: time-shift (verse 1 = present; verse 2 = past or future of the same situation)
  - move 2: POV-shift (verse 1 = your view; verse 2 = the other person's view)
  - move 3: zoom (verse 1 = close detail; verse 2 = wider context — or the reverse)
  - move 4: consequence (verse 1 = the choice; verse 2 = what happened after)
  - move 5: the body-of-verse-1-with-new-images (same syntactic shape, different concrete nouns)
  - move 6: the cut option (sometimes there shouldn't be a verse 2 — go to bridge or instrumental)
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *Writing Better Lyrics*; Jimmy Webb, *Tunesmith*; cross-link `corpus/lyrics/object-writing-and-sensory-imagery.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `second-verse`, `lyric-development`

### B3. `the-chorus-isnt-lifting`

- **Title:** Stall: The Chorus Isn't Lifting
- **Common_signals:**
  - "my chorus doesn't feel like a chorus"
  - "the chorus is flat"
  - "people aren't going to sing along to this"
  - "the chorus and verse feel the same"
- **Recovery_moves:** 7
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (a chorus has to feel like arrival — usually through some combo of melodic register, harmonic resolution, rhythmic shift, lyrical clarity)
  - move 1: melodic register lift (chorus an octave or fifth above the verse's range center)
  - move 2: harmonic arrival (resolve to I or shift to a more anchored progression after a tension-y verse)
  - move 3: rhythmic shift (verse rhythm is busy / chorus rhythm is long-held; or the reverse)
  - move 4: title-as-chorus (the title hits in the first bar of the chorus; the rest of the chorus develops it)
  - move 5: pre-chorus as launcher (insert a pre-chorus that builds tension so the chorus has somewhere to release to)
  - move 6: instrumentation reveal (a new element enters at the chorus — backing vocals, a counter-melody, a hook line)
  - move 7: the "do less in the verse" trick (when the chorus won't lift, sometimes the verse is too busy)
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison; Jimmy Webb; Switched On Pop chorus analyses; cross-link `corpus/structure/section-function-verse-prechorus-chorus-bridge.md` and `corpus/melody/hook-construction.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `chorus`, `arrangement`

### B4. `the-demo-trap`

- **Title:** Stall: The Demo Trap — Polish Killing Momentum
- **Common_signals:**
  - "I keep tweaking the mix and not writing"
  - "I can't move on from this demo"
  - "I've been mixing this for weeks"
  - "I think the song's done but I can't stop fiddling"
- **Recovery_moves:** 6
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (mixing is comfortable; writing the next song is scary; polish is procrastination disguised as work)
  - move 1: bounce and walk away (commit the current mix; don't open the session for 48 hours)
  - move 2: start the next song before finishing this one (forces commitment by reducing emotional weight)
  - move 3: the "ship in 24 hours" rule (artificial deadline)
  - move 4: get an outside ear (the trap is invisible to you; an honest friend says "it's done")
  - move 5: the parts that are NOT mix decisions (lyric edits, arrangement choices) are the only legitimate non-mix changes remaining; everything else is procrastination
  - move 6: the graveyard-folder option (declare the song done as-is; move it to "finished, may revisit"; start fresh)
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Mixerman, *Zen and the Art of Producing*; Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music* (finishing chapters); cross-link `corpus/workflow/finishing-work-and-completion.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `demo-trap`, `procrastination`, `finishing-psychology`

### B5. `the-arrangement-freeze`

- **Title:** Stall: The Arrangement Freeze — Too Many Ideas
- **Common_signals:**
  - "I have too many ideas and can't pick"
  - "every section sounds different"
  - "the song has no through-line"
  - "I keep adding parts and it's getting worse"
- **Recovery_moves:** 6
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (every idea sounds good in isolation; the song needs subtraction, not addition)
  - move 1: declare the through-line (one sentence — what is this song about?)
  - move 2: mute everything; add back one part per pass
  - move 3: the "if you had to cut half the parts, which half" exercise
  - move 4: the bus / send architecture as decision (group similar ideas into a single bus; if a bus is muted and the song works, that bus is cuttable)
  - move 5: outside-ear ABing (which version do other people prefer?)
  - move 6: the "make a 3-stem version" — vocals + one harmonic instrument + one rhythmic element; if that version is the song, the rest was clutter
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Rick Rubin, *The Creative Act* (subtraction chapters); cross-link A5 (Rubin reduction workflow); `corpus/structure/arrangement-arcs-and-transitions.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `arrangement`, `subtraction`

### B6. `lyric-doesnt-mean-anything`

- **Title:** Stall: The Lyric Doesn't Mean Anything
- **Common_signals:**
  - "I don't know what this song is about anymore"
  - "the lyric feels generic"
  - "I'm just rhyming words"
  - "no one would care about this lyric"
- **Recovery_moves:** 7
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (the writer lost the why; the lyric is now craft without subject)
  - move 1: the "letter method" pivot (write the song as a letter to a real person — cross-link A1)
  - move 2: object writing as recovery (Pattison's exercise — 10 minutes of sensory imagery; the song's missing concrete details may surface)
  - move 3: the "what do you actually want to say" out-loud test (record yourself talking about it; transcribe; that's the song)
  - move 4: declare the title (the title declares the subject; if you can't title it, you don't know what it's about)
  - move 5: read the lyric aloud and stop at the first abstract claim (replace with a concrete image)
  - move 6: the cut option (some songs aren't worth finishing — when to abandon)
  - move 7: the "specificity-first" pivot (cross-link A7 — find one concrete image and excavate)
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *Writing Better Lyrics*; Aimee Mann interviews; Nick Cave, *The Red Hand Files*; cross-link C1, C2, C8, A1, A7
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `lyric`, `meaning`, `revision`

### B7. `i-hate-this-song-now`

- **Title:** Stall: The "I Hate This Song Now" Reset
- **Common_signals:**
  - "I used to like this song and now I hate it"
  - "I've heard it too many times"
  - "I can't tell if it's good anymore"
  - "everything about this song annoys me"
- **Recovery_moves:** 6
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (repetition-blindness; the song is fine, your ear has gone dead; or it's not fine, and the dead ear is honest)
  - move 1: separation (don't open the session for a week; come back fresh)
  - move 2: the outside-ear test (does anyone else hear what you hear?)
  - move 3: the "name three specific things you hate" exercise (vagueness is dead-ear; specifics are real problems)
  - move 4: the partial-rewrite option (keep the chorus, rewrite the verses — or the inverse)
  - move 5: change of context (different speakers, different room, different time of day — fresh ear sometimes lives there)
  - move 6: the abandon decision (sometimes the song is dead — graveyard it and write a new one)
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music* (fresh-ears chapters); Sodajerker on songwriters' "I hated it then loved it" stories; cross-link F5
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `aversion`, `fresh-ears`, `finishing-psychology`

### B8. `abandoning-vs-persisting`

- **Title:** Stall: Abandoning vs Persisting — When to Call It
- **Common_signals:**
  - "should I keep going or scrap this"
  - "is this song worth finishing"
  - "I don't know if it's worth my time"
  - "is this a real song or just an idea"
- **Recovery_moves:** 5
- **Coverage points:**
  - why it stalls (the writer is trying to make the decision without a framework)
  - move 1: the "core idea" test (is there one concrete element — a hook, a line, an image, a chord move — that you're proud of? if yes, finish around it. if no, this might be a sketch not a song)
  - move 2: the "what would it cost to finish" estimate (hours vs. the value of the result)
  - move 3: the "what's the next move" test (if you can name the next concrete move, do it. if you can't, you're not stuck — you're done)
  - move 4: the graveyard-folder practice (archive don't delete; some songs come back years later)
  - move 5: the opportunity cost — finishing this vs. starting a fresh one (your time is the constraint; the choice is the cost)
  - Socratic prompts
- **Canonical references:** Steven Pressfield, *The War of Art* (Resistance); Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music*; Cal Newport on creative production; cross-link F5
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `stall-recovery`, `abandonment`, `decision-framework`, `finishing-psychology`

---

## C. Lyric Craft — `corpus/songwriting/lyric/`

### C1. `show-dont-tell-concrete-noun-discipline`

- **Title:** Show Don't Tell — The Concrete-Noun Discipline
- **Subtopics:**
  - the principle (concrete nouns + action verbs land; abstractions slide off)
  - the Aimee Mann / Lucinda Williams / John Prine school
  - the noun-verb-noun diagnostic (every line should have a concrete noun, an action verb, and another concrete noun)
  - the "test the line by reading it without context" check
  - the trap of abstract emotion words (love, sadness, freedom) — when they earn their place vs. when they're filler
  - concrete-but-clichéd nouns (roses, rain) — when they're fresh vs. dead
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *Writing Better Lyrics*; Aimee Mann interviews; Lucinda Williams interviews in Bill Friskics-Warren's *I'll Take You There*; cross-link `corpus/lyrics/object-writing-and-sensory-imagery.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `show-dont-tell`, `concrete-noun`, `mann`, `williams`, `prine`

### C2. `specificity-vs-abstraction-when-each-lands`

- **Title:** Specificity vs Abstraction — When Each Lands
- **Subtopics:**
  - the default (specificity beats abstraction in 90% of pop)
  - the exception (Eilish "Happier Than Ever" specifics; Swift's named-objects on *folklore*) — specificity at scale
  - the inverse (when abstraction is the move — anthems, declarations, "We Are the World" territory)
  - the universal-via-specific principle (your one detail makes everyone feel theirs)
  - cliché as failed abstraction (a tired metaphor — what to do instead)
  - the "if you cut the proper noun" test (does the line still mean something?)
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison; Sheila Davis, *The Craft of Lyric Writing*; Switched On Pop episodes on Taylor Swift / Billie Eilish lyric craft
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `specificity`, `abstraction`, `lyric-decision`

### C3. `revision-discipline-the-dylan-arc`

- **Title:** Revision Discipline — The Dylan Arc
- **Subtopics:**
  - the premise (revision is the craft; the first draft is the seed)
  - Dylan's notebook archive (cross-link A13) — same song through 5+ drafts
  - the "every line earns its place" rule
  - the line-by-line revision pass
  - the verse-by-verse revision pass (different scale — does each verse develop the previous?)
  - the "read aloud, mark the false notes" technique
  - the trap of over-revision (when you've revised the life out of a draft)
  - the time-between-passes discipline (let it sit; come back; revise; let it sit)
- **Canonical references:** *The Bob Dylan Archive* (Tulsa); Cameron Crowe's Dylan interviews; David Hajdu, *Positively 4th Street*; Pat Pattison's revision chapters; cross-link A13
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `revision`, `dylan`, `discipline`

### C4. `internal-rhyme-slant-multi-syllabic`

- **Title:** Internal Rhyme, Slant Rhyme, Multi-Syllabic Schemes
- **Subtopics:**
  - the rhyme taxonomy (perfect, slant/near, family, assonance, consonance, multi-syllabic, chain)
  - cadential strength per rhyme type (perfect = closure; slant = motion; chain = energy)
  - internal rhyme (rhyme within a line) and how it accelerates pace
  - the multi-syllabic / chain rhyme tradition (Eminem, Black Thought, Pusha T, MF DOOM)
  - matching rhyme density to section function (sparse verses, dense chorus or vice versa)
  - the trap of forced perfect rhyme (rhyming "fire" with "desire" — when it's tired)
  - genre boundedness (folk rewards slant; hip-hop rewards complexity)
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *The Essential Guide to Rhyming*; Pattison, *Writing Better Lyrics*; *How to Rap* (Paul Edwards) for the hip-hop tradition; cross-link `corpus/lyrics/rhyme-types-and-cadence-function.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `rhyme`, `internal-rhyme`, `multi-syllabic`, `hip-hop`

### C5. `meter-prosody-syllable-stress`

- **Title:** Meter, Prosody, Syllable Stress Against the Bar Line
- **Subtopics:**
  - the principle (the stressed syllable should land on the strong beat; the weak on the weak)
  - the natural-stress-of-English rule (no forcing — let the words breathe)
  - the syllable-count constraint per line (and when to break it for effect)
  - the verse vs. chorus prosodic contrast (verses often busier rhythmically; choruses often longer-held)
  - Pattison's prosody framework (form mirrors content — unstable feeling = asymmetric form)
  - the "say it out loud" diagnostic (where do you naturally stress? does the melody agree?)
  - the case for deliberate stress-mismatch (Sufjan, Bob Dylan, Joanna Newsom break the rule for character)
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *Writing Better Lyrics*; Pattison, *Songwriting: Essential Guide to Lyric Form and Structure*; cross-link `corpus/lyrics/prosody-and-form-content-alignment.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `meter`, `prosody`, `stress`, `pattison`

### C6. `image-stacking-technique`

- **Title:** Image-Stacking Technique — The Sufjan / Bird / Newsom School
- **Subtopics:**
  - the principle (stack 3–5 concrete sensory images per verse without explicit logical connective tissue — the listener fills in)
  - the Sufjan Stevens approach (*Illinois* verses; *Carrie & Lowell* grief imagery)
  - Andrew Bird's image cascades
  - Joanna Newsom's narrative-via-images
  - the risk (incoherence — when image-stacking reads as random)
  - the discipline (each image should be tonally consistent with the song's emotional center)
  - the "if the listener can paint it, you did it right" test
  - when this works (atmosphere-focused song) and when it doesn't (story song with plot beats)
- **Canonical references:** Sufjan interviews in *The Believer*, *Pitchfork*; Andrew Bird interviews in *Bandcamp Daily*; Joanna Newsom interview archive; cross-link `corpus/lyrics/object-writing-and-sensory-imagery.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `image-stacking`, `sufjan`, `bird`, `newsom`

### C7. `narrative-voice-pov-decisions`

- **Title:** Narrative Voice — POV Decisions
- **Subtopics:**
  - the four POVs (first person / second person / third person / direct address)
  - what each does emotionally (first = confession; second = accusation or seduction; third = story; direct address = a letter)
  - the "you" question (the lover, the listener, the self, God — different "you"s)
  - tense decisions (past = reflective; present = immediate; future = anticipatory)
  - when to switch POV mid-song (and when not to)
  - the unreliable narrator (Father John Misty, Randy Newman — character songs)
  - the trap of unintended POV slip
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison; Sheila Davis, *The Craft of Lyric Writing*; Randy Newman interviews on character-writing; cross-link `corpus/lyrics/point-of-view-tense-and-narrator.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `pov`, `narrator`, `tense`

### C8. `theme-consistency-checks`

- **Title:** Theme Consistency — Stress-Testing Lines Against the Song's Subject
- **Subtopics:**
  - the principle (declare the theme; every line must serve it)
  - the "what is this song actually about" exercise (one sentence)
  - the line-by-line stress test (each line answered against the theme)
  - the trap of off-theme cleverness (a brilliant line that doesn't belong)
  - revision via cutting (often the cut line was the off-theme one)
  - the case for productive ambiguity (the song's theme can be multiple things; the test is intentionality)
  - the "title declares the theme" rule (if you can't title the song, you don't know its subject)
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison; Jimmy Webb, *Tunesmith*; Paul Zollo, *Songwriters on Songwriting*
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `theme`, `revision`, `coherence`

### C9. `refrain-construction-earning-the-title`

- **Title:** Refrain Construction — Earning the Title
- **Subtopics:**
  - the title as the song's center of gravity
  - the title-in-the-chorus convention (and when to break it)
  - the "earning" — by the time the listener hears the title repeated, they should understand its weight
  - first-line-as-title vs. last-line-as-title vs. title-as-refrain
  - the "title hits in bar 1" rule and its exceptions
  - the title-as-question (Beatles "Norwegian Wood (This Bird Has Flown)")
  - the title-as-answer (Pulp "Common People")
  - the trap of a great title with no song
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison; Jimmy Webb, *Tunesmith* (the title-as-anchor chapters); Switched On Pop title analyses; Sheila Davis
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `title`, `refrain`, `hook`

### C10. `co-writing-lyric-dynamics`

- **Title:** Co-Writing Lyric Dynamics — The Room
- **Subtopics:**
  - the holding-the-pen question (who actually types)
  - the veto-vs-build convention (any writer can veto; the proposer can ask "what if instead")
  - the "yes-and" opening, the "is this earning its place" close
  - sensitivity zones — when a lyric is too personal for co-write (and what to do)
  - the split-sheet protocol (writers' percentages at session end)
  - finishing in the room vs. taking the song home
  - co-writing for a third party (a "cut" for an artist who isn't in the room)
  - the publisher and tape-day downstream
- **Canonical references:** Holly Gleason archive; Jason Blume, *Six Steps to Songwriting Success*; Nashville Songwriters Association International (NSAI) materials; cross-link A15
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `lyric-craft`, `co-writing`, `nashville`, `protocol`

---

## D. Melodic & Harmonic Moves — `corpus/songwriting/melodic-harmonic/`

### D1. `modal-interchange-by-mood`

- **Title:** Modal Interchange by Mood — The Move-to-Mood Map
- **Subtopics:**
  - what modal interchange means (borrowing from parallel modes)
  - what ♭III feels like (yearning / nostalgic — Beatles "Let It Be" sections)
  - what ♭VI feels like (uplifting-but-melancholy — Coldplay catalog)
  - what ♭VII feels like (anthemic / rock — Tom Petty catalog)
  - what minor iv feels like (heartbreaking resolution — *Creep*, *Stairway*)
  - the trap of mood-shopping (using a borrowed chord because it sounds cool, not because it fits)
  - the "earn the borrowing" rule
  - cross-link the general-corpus modal-interchange file for the theory layer
- **Canonical references:** Dominic Pedler, *Songwriting Secrets of the Beatles*; Rick Beato, *The Beato Book*; Open Music Theory v2 Chromaticism unit; cross-link `corpus/harmony/modal-interchange-and-borrowed-chords.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `modal-interchange`, `mood`, `harmony`

### D2. `modulation-moves-between-sections`

- **Title:** Modulation Moves Between Sections
- **Subtopics:**
  - the half-step-up modulation (Whitney Houston "I Will Always Love You" final chorus; the "truck driver's gear shift")
  - the fifth-up modulation (Beatles "Penny Lane" verse-to-chorus; harmonically natural)
  - the parallel-key modulation (major to parallel minor; *Bohemian Rhapsody*)
  - the deceptive-resolution-as-modulation (resolve to vi instead of I, then build from there)
  - the pivot-chord modulation (the academic move applied to pop)
  - modulation for energy vs. modulation for emotion
  - the "key change can save a flat bridge" rule
  - when modulation is the wrong move (sometimes the song wants to stay)
- **Canonical references:** Dominic Pedler; *The Beato Book*; Open Music Theory v2 Modulation unit; Switched On Pop modulation episodes
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `modulation`, `key-change`, `arrangement`

### D3. `hook-construction-patterns`

- **Title:** Hook Construction Patterns
- **Subtopics:**
  - hook types (melodic, lyrical, rhythmic, production)
  - the four-bar hook archetype (and its variations — 2-bar, 8-bar)
  - repetition-with-variation (the second-iteration trick — same melody, one note changed)
  - the answer-phrase (call → response within the hook itself)
  - the post-chorus hook (instrumental hook after the chorus — modern pop)
  - title-as-hook vs. melodic-hook-without-title
  - the "hum-test" — can you hum it after one listen?
  - the trap of multiple competing hooks (one section, one hook)
- **Canonical references:** Jack Perricone, *Great Songwriting Techniques*; Dominic Pedler; Hooktheory database; Switched On Pop hook analyses; cross-link `corpus/melody/hook-construction.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `hook`, `melody`, `chorus`

### D4. `vocal-range-management`

- **Title:** Vocal Range Management — Writing for the Singer's Actual Range
- **Subtopics:**
  - know-the-singer-first principle (range, sweet spot, break)
  - the verse-as-conversation range (the speaking-voice register)
  - the chorus-as-lift range (above the speaking-voice register)
  - the bridge-as-departure range (often higher or sharply lower)
  - the pre-chorus as range-launcher (climbs to launch the chorus's higher register)
  - the "highest note" placement (one or two per song, save for the moment that matters)
  - the singer's-break consideration (avoiding the break note or featuring it deliberately)
  - cross-writing for vs. writing-for-self
- **Canonical references:** Jimmy Webb, *Tunesmith* (range chapters); voice-teacher resources (Jeannie Deva, Seth Riggs); Switched On Pop range analyses
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `vocal-range`, `range`, `register`, `singer`

### D5. `melodic-contour-shapes`

- **Title:** Melodic Contour Shapes — Section-by-Section Planning
- **Subtopics:**
  - the four contour archetypes (arch, descent, plateau, ascent)
  - verse vs. chorus contour conventions (verse often descending or arched; chorus often arched or plateau-high)
  - contrast between sections — the planning principle
  - the "where does the highest note live" decision
  - intervallic vocabulary (step vs. leap and what each implies)
  - the answering-phrase contour (rising question, descending answer)
  - the trap of unconscious contour repetition (every section ends on the same shape)
  - cross-link the general-corpus contour file
- **Canonical references:** Jack Perricone, *Melody in Songwriting*; Pat Pattison; cross-link `corpus/melody/melodic-contour-and-motif-development.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `contour`, `melody`, `arrangement`

### D6. `pedal-tones-drones-anchoring`

- **Title:** Pedal Tones and Drones — Anchoring Devices
- **Subtopics:**
  - pedal tone definition (a sustained note while harmony changes above it)
  - common pedals (tonic pedal in bridge; dominant pedal building back to a chorus)
  - the drone as production element (Velvet Underground, modal folk, modern Beach House)
  - the "any chord works over a pedal" risk (when pedal becomes lazy)
  - the inverse — moving bass line under a static melody
  - modal anchoring vs. tonal anchoring (when the pedal IS the tonic vs. when it's a tension)
  - the bagpipe-school drone in modern records
- **Canonical references:** Walter Piston, *Harmony* (pedal-point chapter); *The Beato Book*; Switched On Pop on drone in pop (*Blackbird*, *Sea of Love*, modern folk)
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `pedal-tone`, `drone`, `anchoring`

### D7. `voice-leading-basics-for-chord-writers`

- **Title:** Voice Leading Basics — For Chord-Writers, Not Composition Students
- **Subtopics:**
  - the principle (move the fewest notes the smallest distance between chords)
  - common-tone connection (hold the shared note, move the others by step)
  - contrary motion between outer voices
  - the avoidance of parallel fifths/octaves between outer voices (the practical reason — it sounds like one voice doubled)
  - smoothing inversions to minimize voice jumps
  - voice leading on guitar (the "shape adjacent to the shape" trick)
  - voice leading in pad/synth arrangements (top-line stays smooth)
  - cross-link the general-corpus voice-leading file
- **Canonical references:** Open Music Theory v2 Unit IV; Walter Piston; *The Beato Book*; cross-link `corpus/harmony/voice-leading-for-songwriters.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `voice-leading`, `chord-writing`, `arrangement`

### D8. `chord-substitutions-for-mood-shift`

- **Title:** Chord Substitutions for Mood-Shift — Jazz-Derived Moves in Pop
- **Subtopics:**
  - the tritone substitution (♭II7 for V7) — the unexpected resolution
  - the IV-becomes-iv move (the canonical sad turn — *Wonderwall*, countless pop ballads)
  - ♭II°7 as the dramatic predominant
  - the "secondary dominant chain" (V/V → V → I) — driving forward motion
  - the deceptive-resolution mood (V → vi instead of I)
  - the iim7♭5 substitution (jazz minor predominant in pop)
  - the trap of move-shopping (substitution because it's interesting, not because the song wants it)
  - cross-link general-corpus modal-interchange and secondary-dominants files
- **Canonical references:** Mark Levine, *The Jazz Theory Book*; Dominic Pedler; *The Beato Book*; cross-link `corpus/harmony/modal-interchange-and-borrowed-chords.md` and `corpus/harmony/secondary-dominants-and-applied-chords.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `melodic-harmonic`, `substitution`, `jazz-in-pop`, `mood-shift`

---

## E. Form Decisions — `corpus/songwriting/form/`

### E1. `aaba-vs-verse-chorus-decision`

- **Title:** AABA vs Verse-Chorus — The Decision Framework
- **Subtopics:**
  - the AABA form (32-bar Tin Pan Alley; Beatles' early catalog; "Yesterday")
  - the verse-chorus form (60s onward dominant)
  - when AABA still wins (story-songs, jazz standards, Stephen Sondheim, certain folk)
  - when verse-chorus is the default (modern pop, radio, streaming)
  - the hybrid forms (verse-chorus with AABA-like bridge as the B)
  - what each form *believes* about how songs work (AABA = melody-as-king; verse-chorus = hook-as-king)
  - era-boundedness (AABA was the default through the 50s; verse-chorus dominates 1965+)
  - cross-link the general-corpus pop-song-forms file
- **Canonical references:** Pat Pattison, *Songwriting: Essential Guide to Lyric Form and Structure*; Jack Perricone, *Great Songwriting Techniques*; Allan Moore, *Song Means*; cross-link `corpus/structure/pop-song-forms.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `form-decision`, `aaba`, `verse-chorus`, `form`

### E2. `section-lengths-by-genre-and-era`

- **Title:** Section Lengths by Genre and Era
- **Subtopics:**
  - the 8-bar verse / 8-bar chorus baseline (radio pop, 1960s onward)
  - the 16-bar verse tradition (folk, country, narrative songs)
  - the 12-bar blues structure as exception
  - the streaming-era shortening (intros ≤15s; verses sometimes 4-bar; sub-3-min songs)
  - the post-chorus expansion (chorus + post-chorus → effectively a 16-bar chorus block)
  - hip-hop bar conventions (16-bar verse; 8-bar hook)
  - country / Nashville section conventions
  - the "match the singer's breath" rule (line length tied to phrasing)
- **Canonical references:** John Seabrook, *The Song Machine*; Switched On Pop episodes on streaming-era song shortening; *Billboard* analyses of song length trends; cross-link `corpus/structure/section-function-verse-prechorus-chorus-bridge.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `form-decision`, `section-length`, `genre`, `era`

### E3. `modern-forms-post-chorus-drop-beat-switch`

- **Title:** Modern Forms — Post-Chorus, Drop, Beat-Switch, Bridge-as-Outro
- **Subtopics:**
  - the post-chorus (the Eilish / Charli / Carpenter / Dua Lipa instrumental-or-stripped hook after the chorus)
  - the drop architecture (EDM verse-build-drop; pop adaptations)
  - the beat-switch (Travis Scott / Kanye / hip-hop standard — the song morphs mid-track)
  - the bridge-as-outro (no bridge; the outro IS the new section)
  - the intro-as-chorus (start with the hook; "Hey Ya")
  - the structure-defying single-section song (Lana Del Rey occasional)
  - when modern forms fit the material vs. when they're chasing trends
  - cross-link the general-corpus arrangement-arcs file
- **Canonical references:** Switched On Pop episodes on post-chorus / drop / beat-switch; John Seabrook, *The Song Machine*; Allan Moore, *Song Means*; cross-link `corpus/structure/arrangement-arcs-and-transitions.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `form-decision`, `post-chorus`, `drop`, `beat-switch`, `modern`

### E4. `intro-outro-discipline`

- **Title:** Intro and Outro Discipline
- **Subtopics:**
  - the cold-open (drop in on the first verse — no intro)
  - the instrumental intro (8-bar / 4-bar / 2-bar — and when each fits)
  - the riff-as-intro (Tom Petty "American Girl"; Stones "Satisfaction")
  - the streaming-era 7-second rule (Spotify skip threshold; intro pressure)
  - outro types (fade, cold cut, tag, instrumental departure, false ending)
  - the "outro should resolve or surprise" decision
  - the dance-music outro (extended mix conventions for DJ blending)
  - the "outro is where you can experiment" rule
- **Canonical references:** Switched On Pop intro/outro episodes; *Billboard* and *Music Business Worldwide* on the streaming-era intro shortening; Allan Moore, *Song Means*
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `form-decision`, `intro`, `outro`, `streaming-era`

### E5. `through-composed-song-construction`

- **Title:** Through-Composed Song Construction
- **Subtopics:**
  - what through-composed means (no repeated sections; the song moves forward without returning)
  - canonical examples (Joanna Newsom *Ys* tracks; Big Thief "Not"; Bowie "Station to Station"; Sufjan "The Only Thing"; queen "Bohemian Rhapsody" sections)
  - what through-composing buys (narrative momentum, no repetition-fatigue, surprise as a structural element)
  - what it costs (no hook to anchor; harder for the listener to grasp; harder to memorize)
  - the discipline (every section must earn its place against the previous and the next)
  - the "where does this song end" decision
  - through-composing in commercial pop (rare but it happens — Frank Ocean "Pyramids")
  - when this fits (epic, prog, indie folk) and when it doesn't (radio pop)
- **Canonical references:** Allan Moore, *Song Means*; Switched On Pop on Joanna Newsom; Sodajerker on Big Thief; cross-link `corpus/structure/pop-song-forms.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `form-decision`, `through-composed`, `narrative`, `experimental`

---

## F. Finishing Psychology — `corpus/songwriting/finishing/`

### F1. `why-songs-dont-get-finished`

- **Title:** Why Songs Don't Get Finished — A Diagnostic
- **Subtopics:**
  - the categories (creative block, perfectionism, scope creep, taste-gap, social fear, no-finish-line ambiguity)
  - the creative-psychology research (Csikszentmihalyi flow; Oettingen on mental contrasting; Pressfield's Resistance)
  - the "you only finish what you ship" rule (Cal Newport on deep work)
  - the songwriting-specific version (DeSantis *Making Music*'s "decide once" principle)
  - the taste-gap diagnosis (Ira Glass on "the gap between your taste and your work")
  - the fear-of-judgment factor
  - the diagnostic tree (which category is this user in? what does the move differ?)
- **Canonical references:** Steven Pressfield, *The War of Art*; Cal Newport, *Deep Work*; Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, *Flow*; Ira Glass talk; Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music*
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `finishing-psychology`, `procrastination`, `creative-block`, `diagnostic`

### F2. `done-better-than-perfect-frameworks`

- **Title:** "Done > Perfect" Frameworks for Songwriting
- **Subtopics:**
  - the maxim's origin (often attributed to Voltaire; popularized by Sheryl Sandberg in tech) and its limits
  - Ira Glass's "taste gap" framework
  - DeSantis's "decide once, move on" — the operational version
  - the "ship date" discipline (declare it; honor it)
  - the 80/20 cutoff and its application to songwriting (when the last 20% isn't worth it)
  - the difference between "done" and "good enough" and "shipped"
  - the perfectionist's reframe (perfect is the enemy of done IS the principle but the perfectionist hears it wrong)
  - the case for NOT shipping ("not every song is ready" vs. "no song is ever ready")
- **Canonical references:** Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music*; Ira Glass talk; Cal Newport; Mixerman, *Zen and the Art of Producing*; cross-link `corpus/workflow/finishing-work-and-completion.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `finishing-psychology`, `done-over-perfect`, `shipping`, `framework`

### F3. `finish-ugly-school`

- **Title:** The Finish-Ugly School — Commit the Demo as the Master
- **Subtopics:**
  - the principle (the demo IS the song; polish kills the feel)
  - canonical examples (Daniel Johnston, Guided By Voices, Springsteen *Nebraska*, *American Recordings*, lo-fi indie tradition, mk.gee aesthetic)
  - the case (the first-take rawness has information; polish discards it)
  - the modern lo-fi/bedroom tradition (Clairo early, Phoebe Bridgers early)
  - the discipline ("the demo will be the master" as a session-opening declaration)
  - the trap (using lo-fi as cover for bad songwriting)
  - the genre-boundedness (works in indie, folk, lo-fi; doesn't work in radio pop)
  - cross-link to Aesthetic-stream lo-fi files if/when they exist
- **Canonical references:** *Trouble Will Find Me* (Springsteen on *Nebraska*); Guided By Voices interview archive; Tape Op archives on lo-fi philosophy; cross-link `corpus/workflow/finishing-work-and-completion.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `finishing-psychology`, `finish-ugly`, `lo-fi`, `commit-the-demo`

### F4. `80-20-finishing-rule-mix-master`

- **Title:** The 80/20 Finishing Rule — Applied to Mix and Master
- **Subtopics:**
  - the Pareto principle (the last 20% of polish is 80% of the time)
  - the songwriter-mixing-self trap (over-polishing the demo as procrastination)
  - the diminishing-returns curve (each pass moves the needle less)
  - the "outside ear" threshold (when only an outsider can tell the difference, you're done)
  - the cost-benefit framing (would you trade this hour of polishing for an hour of writing the next song?)
  - the "bounce, distance, bounce again" rule (commit to a cut, distance, re-evaluate)
  - the case for going past 80 (only when the song will *carry your name forward* — albums, singles, sync placements)
  - cross-link general-corpus mixing files (the mixing principles themselves live there)
- **Canonical references:** Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music* (the finishing chapters); Mixerman, *Zen and the Art of Producing*; cross-link `corpus/workflow/finishing-work-and-completion.md`
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `finishing-psychology`, `80-20`, `mixing`, `diminishing-returns`

### F5. `when-to-abandon-and-start-over`

- **Title:** When to Abandon and Start Over — Frameworks for the Call
- **Subtopics:**
  - the abandonment framework (sunk cost is real; opportunity cost is real-er)
  - the "core idea" test (cross-link B8 — is there a worth-saving element?)
  - the graveyard-folder practice (archive don't delete; songs come back years later)
  - the partial-rescue option (the verse melody lives; salvage it into a new song)
  - the "abandon the take, not the song" alternative (sometimes rerecording is enough)
  - songwriting-specific timelines (when does a sitting-too-long song count as abandoned?)
  - the "60 finished mediocre songs vs. 6 finished masterpieces" debate (volume-vs-quality framing)
  - the case for finishing bad songs anyway (learning happens at the finish line)
- **Canonical references:** Steven Pressfield, *The War of Art*; Dennis DeSantis, *Making Music*; Cal Newport, *Deep Work*; Sodajerker interviews on abandoned songs; cross-link F1, B8
- **Tags:** `songwriting-craft`, `finishing-songs`, `finishing-psychology`, `abandonment`, `graveyard`, `decision-framework`

---

## Suggested firing order

If you don't want to fire all 51 prompts at once, this priority order builds the most useful retrieval surface first:

**Wave 1 — highest mission-fit (~14 files):**
A1, A2, A3, A5, A8, A10, B1, B2, B3, B4, B6, F1, F2, F5
*(workflow seeds: Cave letter, Lynch fishing, Eno cards, Rubin reduction, beat-first, Cosm 8-bar; stalls: bridge, second-verse, chorus, demo-trap, lyric-meaning; finishing: why, done-over-perfect, abandon)*

**Wave 2 — depth on lyric + harmony + finishing (~14 files):**
A4, A7, A11, C1, C2, C3, C6, D1, D2, D3, E1, E3, F3, F4
*(Bowie cut-up, Mann specificity, Spoon discipline; show-don't-tell, specificity/abstraction, revision, image-stacking; modal-mood, modulation, hooks; AABA-vs-VC, modern forms; finish-ugly, 80/20)*

**Wave 3 — remaining (~23 files):**
A6, A9, A12, A13, A14, A15, B5, B7, B8, C4, C5, C7, C8, C9, C10, D4, D5, D6, D7, D8, E2, E4, E5
*(everything else)*

---

## Post-processing checklist

Standard `PROMPTS.md` checklist plus stream-specific:

- [ ] Corpus file saved at `corpus/songwriting/{bucket}/{filename}.md`
- [ ] **For Bucket A only:** workflow template ALSO saved at `arlo/workflows/{slug}.md` with the required frontmatter (name / origin / type / applicable_when / duration_estimate)
- [ ] **For Bucket B only:** stall file has the required frontmatter (problem / common_signals / recovery_moves)
- [ ] Front-matter source line reads `Deep-research synthesis (2026-05) — Songwriting Craft`
- [ ] Every claim is inline-cited; sources are real URLs, not invented
- [ ] No AI-tutorial sites cited; no genre-fan blogs cited; no anonymous listicles cited
- [ ] When the technique is one school of thought, the school is named
- [ ] When working writers disagree, the disagreement is surfaced
- [ ] Cross-link footer present, pointing at existing general-corpus and other-stream files
- [ ] Tags include `songwriting-craft` and `finishing-songs` at minimum
